title: Nested pediatric low-grade glioma cerebral organoid avatars reveal glutamatergic neuron stromal growth dependency.
abstract: Pilocytic astrocytomas (PAs), the most common pediatric brain tumor, grow slowly and depend heavily on their local brain microenvironment, hindering accurate humanized model development. To surmount this barrier, we developed mixed assembloids of PAs nested within human iPSC-cerebral organoids (PANCOs) that recapitulate PA histopathology and growth. Consistent with their cell-intrinsic ERK dependence, MEK inhibition reduces PANCO proliferation, whereas clozapine-N-oxide-activatable neural progenitors integrate into PANCOs, preferentially differentiate into glutamatergic neurons, and increase cell-extrinsic PA proliferation, which is blocked by glutamate receptor inhibition. These accurate in vitro pediatric PA avatars provide tractable platforms to discover and validate PA cell-intrinsic and cell-extrinsic growth dependencies.
authors: Enquan Xu; Christina Zhang; Fausto J Rodriguez; Sonika Dahiya; Corina Anastasaki; David H Gutmann
methods: Materials and methods Human PA cells Operative specimens from children with NF1-associated and sporadic PA tumors were collected under an approved Human Studies (IRB) protocol at Washington University School of Medicine, and were used to derive primary PA cells ( Anastasaki et al. 2025 ). These PA cells were maintained in uncoated and vented T75 flasks in DMEM medium supplemented with 10% FBS, Glutamax and Pen/Strep, and passaged upon reaching confluence using Accutase. The PA cells were lentivirally infected to express core histone protein H2B-Red Fluorescent Protein (RFP) under the EF1α promoter (RRID201121) within the nucleus, as described below. The cells comprised a mixed population of approximately 80% RFP + / 20% RFP neg  cells, did not undergo selection to maintain primary PA cell heterogeneity, and were passaged at high density once a week. Some PA cells were maintained as spheroids by passaging and pelleting 30,000 cells at the bottom of low-cell attachment U-bottom plates, allowing the tumor cells to aggregate overnight, prior to maintaining the spheroids in low-cell attachment containers under static (non-shaking) conditions. Human cerebral organoids Cerebral organoid cultures were generated as previously described ( Anastasaki et al. 2022b ) with minor adaptations. Human NF1 patient-derived iPSCs heterozygous for an  NF1  mutation ( NF1 Y489C/+ ), iPSCs CRISPR-Cas9-engineered to harbor a heterozygous NF1 patient-derived  NF1  mutation ( NF1 C383X/+ ), and an isogenic control line (BJFF.6) were initially expanded on Matrigel-coated T25 flasks and detached using ReLeSR. Embryoid bodies (EBs) were generated by seeding 40,000 live hiPSCs per well into a 96-well low-attachment plate, followed by centrifugation at 300 × g for 3 minutes to promote cell aggregation. Neural induction was initiated by replacing mTeSR medium with STEMdiff ™  Neural Induction Medium supplemented with 4 ng/mL bFGF and 20 μM ROCK inhibitor. Each well contained 200 μL of media. On day 2, EBs were examined by brightfield microscopy to confirm their formation. Medium was refreshed on day 4 with bFGF and ROCK inhibitor, and again on day 7 without supplements. On day 10, EBs were transferred to ultra-low attachment 24-well plates on an orbital shaker at 80 rpm in a humidified incubator (37°C, 5% CO 2 ) and were maintained in organoid culture media:1: DMEM/F12: Neurobasal ™  Medium supplemented with antibiotic/antimycotic solution, N-2 supplement, B-27 ™  Plus supplement, GlutaMAX, MEM NEAA (Gibco Thermo Fisher Scientific), humulin (250 μl of 100 U/ml in 1L), and 2-Mercaptoethanol (Sigma; 4 μl in 1L),). After 4 weeks  in vitro  (WIV), mature cerebral organoids (COs) were collected and fixed in 4% paraformaldehyde overnight at 4°C. Fixed organoids were then washed in PBS and placed in 30% sucrose for 48 hours of cryoprotection. To generate frozen sections, COs were placed in a mixture of 1:2 (v/v) Tissue-Tek ®  O.C.T. Compound and 30% sucrose, within 15×15×5 mm plastic molds. Tissues were snap-frozen using a slurry of dry ice and 2-methylbutane. Frozen samples were sectioned at 10 μm thickness using a cryostat, and two sections were mounted per glass slide for analyses. Differentiation and maintenance of induced Neural Progenitor Cells (iNPCs) To induce neural progenitor differentiation, hiPSCs were plated onto poly-L-ornithine (Sigma-Aldrich) and laminin (Fisher Scientific)-coated tissue culture flasks. Cells were initially cultured for 3 days in NPC base media, consisting of 50% DMEM/F12, 50% Neurobasal media, supplemented with N-2 , B-27 ™  Plus , and 2 mM GlutaMax (Gibco, Thermo Fisher Scientific). During this phase, medium was supplemented with 10 ng/mL human LIF, 4 μM CHIR99021, 3 μM SB431542, 2 μM Dorsomorphin, and 0.1 μM Compound E (STEMCELL Technologies) prior to growth for an additional 5 days in the same NPC base medium containing 10 ng/mL LIF, 4 μM CHIR99021, 3 μM SB431542, and 0.1 μM Compound E, but omitting dorsomorphin. Thereafter, iNPCs were maintained in NPC base medium supplemented with 10 ng/mL LIF, 3 μM CHIR99021, and 2 μM SB431542. Medium was refreshed daily, and cells were passaged as required using Accutase (STEMCELL Technologies), according to the manufacturer’s protocol. Lentiviral production and cell transduction Lentiviral particles encoding H2B-RFP (Red Fluorescent Protein, RFP; under the EF-1α promoter; RRID201121) and hM3D (Gq) DREADDs receptor (under the SYN1 promoter; VectorBuilder VB241016-1583wgf) were generated using a standard third-generation packaging system. Plasmid constructs and packaging vectors were co-transfected into HEK293T cells using Fugene HD (Promega), following the manufacturer’s protocol. Viral supernatants were harvested 48 hours post-transfection, passed through a 0.45 μm filter, and immediately applied to either patient-derived PA cells or induced neural progenitor cells (iNPCs). Target cells were exposed to viral supernatants for 72 hours, after which time, infection efficiency was assessed by fluorescence microscopy: RFP +  for PA cells and EGFP +  for iNPCs. Transduced cells were maintained  in vitro  until they were used for co-culture with hCOs. PANCO generation Human iPSC-derived cerebral organoids (hCOs) maintained for a minimum of 4 weeks in 24-well ultra-low attachment plates were assessed for neuronal (TUJ1 + ), neural stem cell (SOX2 + ), and astrocytic (GFAP + ) marker expression. WUPA cells, previously transduced with RFP-expressing lentivirus, were seeded at a density of 10,000-100,000 cells per well in U-bottom 96-well ultra-low attachment plates one day prior to incubation with COs. After 24 hours, PA cells formed compact spheroids. For optimal PANCO generation, aggregates from 10,000 cells were gently dissociated using a 200 μL pipette tip to produce smaller spheroids (<100 μm in diameter). For co-culture experiments, hCOs were resuspended using organoid culture medium and transferred into 96-well plates containing PA spheroids and incubated for 72 hours to facilitate integration. Following this initial period, co-cultured assembloids were returned to the 24-well ultra-low attachment plates and maintained under standard culture conditions for an additional 8 weeks. PA integration was monitored weekly by live-cell imaging of RFP +  PA cells within the PANCOs. If PA-RFP +  cells were not observed after one week, the co-culture procedure was repeated to enhance the likelihood of successful PA cell integration. Integration of hM3D(Gq)-GFP-iNPCs into human cerebral organoids Following infection of iPSC-derived NPCs (iNPCs) with hM3D(Gq)-GFP lentiviral particles, the expression efficiency of hM3D(Gq) DREADDs receptor (under the SYN1 promoter) expression in iNPCs was assessed by fluorescence. Once GFP-positive cells exceeded 80%, iNPCs were dissociated using Accutase, and live cells were counted. iNPCs were then resuspended in cerebral organoid culture medium and plated at a density of 50,000 cells per well into U-bottom 96-well ultra-low attachment plates 1 hour prior to co-culture. Once the iNPCs had settled and formed a pellet, hCOs cultured in 24-well plates were gently transferred onto the iNPC pellet. After a 48-hour incubation period, iNPC-hCOs were returned to the 24-well plates. Successful integration was monitored by live-cell imaging starting at 1-week post-co-culture. If integration was not achieved, the co-culture procedure was repeated once to improve the rate of successful integration. Sequential integration and DREADD activation in PANCOs Human iNPC-hM3D-PANCOs were generated via sequential integration of PAs in iNPC-hCOs. 2-4 WIV iNPC-hCOs were co-cultured with WUPA cells as described above for PA cells. Functional activation of hM3DGq-iNPC-derived cells was initiated 8 weeks after PANCO integration. For DREADD activation, clozapine-N-oxide (CNO, 10 μM) was added to the culture medium for 1 hour, followed by a medium change and a second 1-hour CNO treatment 24 hours later. After a second medium change, PANCOs were maintained for an additional 48 hours before collection and immunohistochemical analysis. For memantine treatments, PANCOs were pre-treated with 10 μM CNO for 1 hour, followed by 24 hours of recovery. A second 1-hour CNO treatment was then applied, followed by 2 μM memantine addition to the media. PANCOs were collected 24 hours after memantine treatment following a total treatment duration of 48 hours. Mirdametinib and trametinib treatments PANCOs were treated with 0.5-1.5μM of mirdametinib (MedChem Express) and 50nM trametinib (MedChem Express), with medium replenished daily for a total of 3 days. Treated PANCOs were then collected for histopathological analyses. hCOs were treated with 1μM of mirdametinib for 72 hours prior to collection for histopathological analyses. Unlabeled WUPA cell monolayer cultures were treated with 0.1-1.0μM of mirdametinib for 24h for proliferation assays and western blot analyses. Immunocytochemistry, immunofluorescence, and immunohistochemistry Adherent WUPA cells were washed and fixed with 4% paraformaldehyde. Fixed cells and frozen sections were permeabilized with 0.5% Triton X-100 in PBS for 10 minutes. After blocking with 10% goat serum, samples were incubated overnight at 4 °C with primary antibodies ( Supplemental Table S1 ) diluted in 2% goat serum, following manufacturer-recommended concentrations. Alexa Fluor-conjugated secondary antibodies (1:200) were then applied, and nuclei counterstained with Hoechst 33258 (Thermo Scientific). Samples were mounted with Immu-Mount (Fisher) and imaged using a Leica DMi1 fluorescence microscope with LAS X software. For synapsin-I and bassoon immunostaining, high-resolution confocal imaging was performed using a ZEISS LSM 880 with Airyscan to assess synaptic proximity between integrated neuronal and PA cells. For DAB immunohistochemical staining, frozen tissues were serially sectioned (5μm) and immunostained with Ki67, GFAP, OLIG2, phospho-ERK1/2, and synaptophysin antibodies ( Supplemental Table S1 ) using the Vectastain ABC kit and appropriate biotinylated secondary antibodies. Proximity Ligation Assays Proximity ligation assays (PLAs) were performed to detect protein-protein interactions between PDGFRA:TUJ1, homer::bassoon, homer::PDGFRA, and PDGFRA::bassoon. Each assay was conducted using the Duolink ®  In Situ Detection Kit (Millipore Sigma, Cat# DUO92013) according to the manufacturer’s specifications. Briefly, all reagents were freshly prepared. PANCO tissue cryosections were washed and blocked using Duolink Blocking Solution, Appropriate primary antibodies ( Supplemental Table S1 ) were diluted 1:100 in Duolink Antibody Diluent. After the staining was complete, the slides were mounted using Duolink Mounting Medium containing DAPI before undergoing confocal imaging. Western blotting Treated WUPA cells were collected, snap-frozen, and lysed in protease/phosphatase inhibitor-supplemented RIPA buffer for subsequent western blotting as previously described ( Anastasaki et al., 2025 ). Briefly, 20μg of each sample was blotted using pERK1/2, total ERK1/2 and GAPDH primary and appropriate HRP- and NIR-conjugated secondary antibodies ( Supplemental Table S1 ). Images were captured and analyzed using the Li-Cor Image Studio Lite Version 5.2 software. Resazurin proliferation assays 30,000 WUPA cells were seeded in each well of a 96-well plate 24h prior to treatment vehicle (DMSO) or 0.1-1μM mirdametinib for a total of 24h. During the last 6 h of treatment, 10%v/v resazurin (ThermoFisher) was added to each well, following manufacturer’s instructions. Metabolized resazurin produces resorufin as PA cells proliferate. Resorufin fluorescence was analyzed on a Varioskan ™  LUX Multimode Microplate Reader (ThermoFisher) plate reader using SkanIt (ThermoFisher) software. Quantification and statistical analyses Statistical analyses were performed using GraphPad Prism 5. Depending on the comparison, either two-tailed Student’s T-tests or one-way ANOVA with Dunnett’s or Tukey’s post hoc corrections were used. Statistical significance was defined as p < 0.05, with exact p values reported in the corresponding figure panels. Each analysis included data from a minimum of three independently generated biological replicates. Each biological replicate represented the average of a minimum of two technical replicates. Materials availability Further information and requests for the unique WUPA or human induced pluripotent stem cell lines used in this study should be directed to the lead contact, David H. Gutmann, MD, PhD ( gutmannd@wustl.edu ). These unique materials will be made available on request, but we may require payment and will require a completed Materials Transfer Agreement.
acknowledgements: Acknowledgements. This work was partially funded by grants from the National Institute of Neurological Disorders and Stroke (R35NS07211-01 and R35NS142919 to D.H.G.), National Cancer Institute (1-R50-CA233164-01 to C.A.), and the Gilbert Family Foundation Next Generation Models Initiative (to D.H.G.). We thank Corrine Gardner and the Pediatric Neurosurgery Tissue Bank for coordinating the acquisition of fresh operative specimens used to derive the WUPA cell lines. The Genome Editing and iPSCs Core Facility at Washington University School of Medicine engineered the hiPSCs and is partially subsidized by National Cancer Institute, USA Cancer Center Support grant (P30-CA091842). This publication is solely the responsibility of the authors and does not necessarily represent the official view of NCRR or NIH.  Figures 2A ,  2B ,  2H  and  4A  were created with  BioRender.com .
introduction: Introduction Brain tumors are the most common solid tumor in children, with low-grade pilocytic astrocytomas (PAs) comprising the majority of these cancers ( Fangusaro et al. 2024 ). Unlike their malignant counterparts, World Health Organization grade 1 PAs are typically non-fatal tumors and exhibit unique histopathologic features (piloid morphology with fibrous processes and microcysts, biphasic tissue architecture with some multinucleated cells) ( Tihan T 2021 ), harbor few genomic alterations ( KIAA1549: BRAF  fusion or bi-allelic  NF1  loss), low proliferative rates (1-4% Ki67 +  cells), and a high dependence on their local microenvironment for support ( Pfister et al. 2008 ;  Raabe et al. 2011 ;  Jones et al. 2013 ;  Pan et al. 2018 ;  Buhl et al. 2019 ;  Chen et al. 2019 ). In this regard, we and others have successfully generated primary human PA cell lines, which, in many circumstances, fail to propagate due to premature cellular senescence ( Raabe et al. 2011 ;  Buhl et al. 2019 ;  Selt et al. 2023 ) or grow for a limited time (5-7 days) as xenografts in zebrafish larvae ( Yuan et al. 2021 ). For this reason, much of our understanding of the pathobiology of PAs derives from the use of the only authenticated preclinical platforms that model low-grade optic pathway gliomas arising in children with NF1 ( Bajenaru et al. 2003 ;  Zhu et al. 2005 ). These models have been useful for drug discovery and translation ( Hegedus et al. 2008 ;  Kaul et al. 2015 ), but their applicability to sporadic PAs or those tumors arising in other brain locations is limited. In an effort to generate humanized models of sporadic PA and NF1-associated PA arising outside of the optic pathway, we previously leveraged both human induced pluripotent stem cell (hiPSC) engineering ( Anastasaki et al. 2022b ) and human PA tumor xenografting into immunocompromised  Rag1 -deficient or  Cxcl10 -null mice  in vivo  ( Anastasaki et al. 2022a ). While  NF1 -null or  KIAA1549:BRAF -expressing hiPSC-derived progenitors and human primary PA lines form low-grade gliomas in mice  in vivo , the resulting tumors fail to exhibit some of the characteristic histological characteristics of the native human tumors, such as eosinophilic granular bodies and Rosenthal fibers. Capitalizing upon the successes of adult (glioblastoma) and pediatric (diffuse midline glioma) malignant brain tumor organoid modeling ( Golebiewska et al. 2020 ;  Pine et al. 2020 ;  Mangena et al. 2025 ), we sought to develop tumor-cerebral organoid assembloids and authenticate them as accurate  in vitro  models of pediatric PA. Following extensive experimental optimization, we generated mixed PA-human iPSC-derived cerebral organoid assembloids (PANCOs) as avatars of both NF1-associated and sporadic pediatric PA. Similar to human PAs, the tumor cells in these PANCOs mimic some of characteristic histopathological features of their native counterparts, exhibit cell-intrinsic MEK-driven growth dependency, and are inhibited by MEK inhibitors (mirdametinib, trametinib) currently in clinical trials [ NCT04923126 ;  NCT02684058 ;  NCT02124772 ]. Moreover, the integration of Designer Receptors Exclusively Activated by Designer Drugs (DREADD)-activatable neural progenitors into these PANCOs reveals preferential glutamatergic neuron generation and tumor infiltration, as well as a cell-extrinsic neuronal glutamate-driven PA growth dependency.
results: Results and Discussion To develop a human preclinical platform recapitulating human PA histopathology for the evaluation and targeting of PA cell-intrinsic and cell-extrinsic growth dependencies, we generated mixed 3D assembloids composed of  PA  cells  N ested within human iPSC-derived  C erebral  O rganoids (PANCOs). As the majority of PAs arise either sporadically harboring  KIAA1549:BRAF  fusion alterations, or in individuals with Neurofibromatosis type 1 (NF1) cancer predisposition syndrome with germline and somatic  NF1  mutations, we employed a series of sporadic (WUPA8, WUPA11) and NF1-associated (WUPA3, WUPA4) primary PA cell lines ( W ashington  U niversity  P ilocytic  A strocytomas, WUPAs) generated from operative specimens at St Louis Children’s Hospital ( Anastasaki et al. 2025 ) ( Fig. 1A ). The PA cells were lentivirally-infected to express nuclear-targeted red fluorescent protein (RFP) under the control of the EF1α promoter. These PA cells were not 100% RFP positive, as they purposely did not undergo antibiotic (e.g., puromycin) selection in order to preserve some of the PA cell population heterogeneity. Additionally, WUPA cells were strongly immunopositive for GFAP, a small portion (<10%) were faintly immunopositive for OLIG2, and were all negative for synaptophysin (neuronal marker;  Fig. 1B ) expression, as is typical for these tumors ( Rodriguez et al. 2008 ;  Tihan T 2021 ). To model sporadic PA tumors, we leveraged a commercially available control (BJFF.6) human iPSC line, while isogenic CRISPR-CAS9-engineered or patient-derived heterozygous  NF1 -mutant human iPSCs ( Anastasaki et al. 2022b ) were used to recapitulate the genetics of NF1-PA ( Fig. 1C ). hiPSCs were differentiated into human cerebral organoids (hCOs), which spontaneously formed organized ventricular zones (SOX2 +  cells) comprised of neurons (TUJ-1 +  cells) and astrocytes (GFAP +  cells) after 4 WIV ( Fig. 1D ), as previously described ( Anastasaki et al. 2022b ). To generate PANCOs in which PA cells are fully encapsulated within the hCO parenchyma, we performed numerous optimization experiments that included modifying the (1) co-culture methods, (2) starting PA cell number and density, (3) age of the host hCOs, and (4) co-culture conditions.  First , we changed the conditions of the PA- hCO co-culture for the first 48 hours. In one approach, we started with PA cells grown as a single-cell suspension in low cell-attachment plates, before introducing 4WIV hCOs, and incubating the co-culture on an orbital shaker at 80rpm for 48h. In the second approach, we started with PA cells pelleted onto U-bottom low-cell attachment plates, prior to transferring 4WIV hCOs directly on top of the pellet and proceeding with a static co-culture for the following 48h. In both cases, post-48h co-cultured hCOs/PAs were transferred to new low-cell attachment multi-well plates and maintained for an additional 2 weeks in vitro (WIV) under continuous orbital shaking ( Fig. 2A ). We found that, while floating cell co-cultures did not produce any PANCOs, initial static co-cultures generated three types of hCO-PA assembloids: (1) fused hCO-PA cell assembloids, which were only connected on a single plane, (2) hCO-PA cell assembloids with small integrated PA areas within hCOs (integrated small; IS), or (3) PANCOs, where PA cells formed tumors within the hCO parenchyma and occupied >2% of the hCO area ( Fig. 2B ).  Second , we altered the initial amount of PA cells used for static co-culture with 4-WIV hCOs (1x10 4 , 3x10 4 , or 1x10 5 ) and found that only co-culturing hCOs with 1x10 4  cells generated PANCOs ( Fig. 2C ,  Supplemental Fig. S1A ).  Third , we modified the age of the recipient hCO from 4-WIV to 12-WIV and determined that co-culture with 4-WIV hCOs was required for efficient PANCO generation ( Fig. 2D ,  Supplemental Fig. S1B ).  Fourth , we changed the duration of the initial co-culture of 1x10 4  PA cells with recipient 4-WIV hCOs (48h, 72h, or 96h) and found that 72hrs of initial infusion was optimal for successful PANCO generation (75% PANCOs) after 8 weeks of co-culture ( Fig. 2E ,  Supplemental Fig. S1D ). Of note, this 72h infusion duration period was selected, as earlier transfers from static cultures to the orbital shaker sheared the PANCOs, while longer infusion periods resulted in PANCOs that were large and could not be maintained successfully.  Fifth , to define the importance of the PA pellet diameter during the initial co-culture period, we minced the 1x10 4  PA cell pellets into 100μm spheroids and found an improved rate of PANCO generation (86%) after 8 weeks of co-culture ( Fig. 2F ).  Sixth , we varied the co-culture duration of the minced spheroids from 1- to 8- WIV ( Fig. 2F ,  Supplemental Fig. S1D ) and determined that the 8-WIV co-culture period was optimal for robust PANCO generation. Additionally, initial co-culture with minced PA cell spheroids resulted in PANCOs nested within healthy hCOs containing intact ventricular zones (SOX2 + ) and differentiated neurons (TUJ1 + ), as opposed to PANCOs generated from a single large PA cell pellet, which had no discernable hCO features with few SOX2- or TUJ1-immunopositive cells ( Fig. 2G ). Taken together, we experimentally determined that generation of reproducible (>80%) PANCOs, where the PA tumor area is fully surrounded by developing hCO tissue, required that 1x10 4  WUPA cells be pelleted and physically dissociated into 100μm diameter spheroids prior to co-culture with 4-WIV hCOs for 72h, and maintained for an additional 8 WIV ( Fig 2H ). Using this optimized method, we generated PANCOs with similar success rates for 7 separate batches the same hCO / PA cell genotype combination (NF1-PANCO-3;  Supplemental Fig. S1E ). We additionally generated multiple batches of sporadic-PANCOs (Sp-PANCO-1 and Sp-PANCO-2) where control hCOs were co-cultured with sporadic WUPA cells (WUPA8 or WUPA11), as well as NF1-PANCOs (NF1-PANCO-1 and NF1-PANCO-2) in which heterozygous  NF1- mutant ( NF1 C383X/+  or  NF1 Y489C/+ ) hCOs were co-cultured with NF1-PA cells (WUPA3 or WUPA10) to mimic the genetics of their respective clinical counterparts ( Pfister et al. 2008 ;  Gutmann et al. 2013 ) ( Supplemental Fig. S1F – G ). As part of the authentication process, we found that the resulting Sp- and NF1-PANCO tumors recapitulated many of the characteristic histopathological features seen in their parental tumors ( Fig. 3A ): All tumor-bearing PANCOs contained well circumscribed tumoral areas identified by RFP immunopositivity. Similar to typical PA surgical biopsy specimens, the tumors within these PANCOs exhibited a biphasic tissue architecture (densely packed cellular regions interspersed with less compact areas containing cystic components), multiple piloid processes, and some multinucleated cells. Additionally, there were rare mitotic figures, apoptotic bodies, and occasional necrotic areas, mostly observed in the organoid portion of the PANCO ( Rodriguez et al. 2008 ). PANCO tumors also demonstrated strong GFAP immunopositivity, faint OLIG2 immunopositivity, and a lack of synaptophysin (Syn) expression ( Fig. 3B ,  Supplemental Fig. S2A ). It should be noted that the surrounding hCO component of the PANCOs mimics some of the cellular diversity the developing brain, including neurons and many differentiated astrocyte cells, which are also positive for GFAP expression ( Fig 1D ,  Fig 3B ). Following standardization, which ensured consistent production of reproducible PANCOs, an entirely human  ex vivo  platform was available to study PA growth dependencies. Similar to typical PA tumor biopsy specimens, both Sp- and NF1-PANCOs exhibited low proliferative indexes (4% Ki67 +  cells), which remained stable over time (2 weeks to 8 weeks;  Fig. 3C ,  Supplemental Fig. S2B – C ), consistent with the slow growth rates of these tumors in children ( Tu et al. 2018 ). Unlike their malignant counterparts, PANCOs maintained their structural integrity and PA architecture, as well as low proliferative indices (Ki67 +  cells), even after 24 WIV ( Supplemental Fig. S2D ). This is similar to PA monocultures grown as spheroids  in vitro  ( Supplemental Fig. S2E ). Additionally, these PA cells retained immunopositivity for GFAP expression ( Supplemental Fig. S2F ) and ERK activation (phosphorylated ERK 1/2, pERK1/2;  Supplemental Fig. S2G ) after 8-WIV to 24-WIV. Since both  KIAA1549:BRAF  and  NF1 -null NF1-PAs have increased MEK activation due to either dysregulated BRAF activity (sporadic PA) or neurofibromin loss of RAS regulation (NF1-PA), we assessed the ability of two MEK inhibitors, currently in clinical trials for children with low-grade gliomas (mirdametinib [ NCT04923126 ]; trametinib [ NCT02684058 ;  NCT02124772 ] ( Manoharan et al. 2020 ;  Selt et al. 2020 )), to suppress PANCO-PA growth. Both agents reduced ERK1/2 phosphorylation (pERK 1/2) ( Fig. 3D ;  Supplemental Fig. S3A ), as well as NF1-PANCO (Mir, 48% reduction; Tra, 87% reduction in %Ki67 +  cells;  Fig. 3E ,  Supplemental Fig. S3B ) and Sp-PANCO (Mir, 50% reduction in %Ki67 +  cells,  Supplemental Fig. S3C ) tumor cell proliferation, without affecting apoptosis ( Supplemental Fig. S3C – D ). 1μM mirdametinib was selected as the minimum concentration that reduced ERK phosphorylation and cell proliferation in PA cell cultures  in vitro  ( Supplemental Fig. S3B ) without affecting non-PA-containing hCO proliferation ( Supplemental Fig. S3C ). This mirdametinib concentration reduced ERK phosphorylation in both PANCOs ( Fig. 3D ) and hCOs ( Supplemental Fig. S3C ). The finding that MEK inhibitors suppress Sp-PA and NF1-PA cell-intrinsic growth is consistent with prior studies reporting the sensitivity of these tumors to RAF/MEK pathway interruption ( Pfister et al. 2008 ;  Kaul et al. 2012 ;  Kaul et al. 2015 ), which served as preclinical evidence to launch RAF/MEK-targeted clinical trials for children with low-grade glioma ( NCT01089101 ,  NCT03363217 ,  NCT04485559 ,  NCT02684058 ). Future studies using PANCOs could focus on anti-senolytic compounds ( Buhl et al. 2019 ;  Selt et al. 2023 ), dual inhibitor studies (e.g., MEK and mTOR inhibition ( Kaul et al. 2015 )), and pharmacological inhibitors that block other downstream effectors of the RAS/RAF signaling pathway ( Ullrich et al. 2020 ) ( Chen et al. 2025 ). In addition to cell-intrinsic growth dependencies, PA cells are heavily reliant on stromal cues and often exhibit cellular senescence in their absence ( Raabe et al. 2011 ;  Buhl et al. 2019 ). One of the critical cells in the glioma microenvironment is the neuron ( Mancusi and Monje 2023 ), which has been shown to drive pediatric and adult high-grade glioma growth through the elaboration of neurotransmitters ( Venkataramani et al. 2019 ;  Venkatesh et al. 2019 ;  Huang-Hobbs et al. 2023 ;  Taylor et al. 2023 ). Building upon recent studies from our laboratory revealing that glutamatergic neurons regulate PA tumor growth in vitro and in vivo through glutamate receptor signaling ( Anastasaki et al. 2025 ), we modified the PANCO generation pipeline to incorporate genetically engineered iPSC-derived DREADD-expressing neural progenitor cells (iNPCs) within the hCOs prior to PA cell co-culture. These iNPCs were lentivirally infected with hM3DqR-GFP and introduced into hCOs prior to PA cell co-culture. These iNPCs expanded and differentiated into neurons within the host hCOs. Following spontaneous differentiation into neurons  in situ , the iNPC-derived neurons were responsive to clozapine N-oxide (CNO) DREADD-mediated activation ( Fig. 4A ), resulting in PANCOs with traceable GFP +  neurons ( Supplemental Fig. S4A ). To assess the proximity of the iNPC-derived GFP +  neurons to the tumor cells within these PANCOs, we leveraged confocal microscopy: We found GFP/synapsin-I +  and GFP/bassoon +  presynaptic structures surrounding PA cells ( Fig. 4B ,  Supplemental Fig. S4B ). Since the PA cells are labeled with nuclear-targeted EF1α-RFP, the distance between the GFP +  presynaptic neurons and the PA cell surface is likely underestimated. Additionally, we performed several proximity ligation assay sets (PLA) to assess the proximity of GFP +  iNPC-derived neurons (TUJ1 +  cells) expressing the presynaptic protein, bassoon, to PA-RFP +  tumor cells (PDGFRA +  cells) expressing the postsynaptic protein, homer ( Fig. 4C ). Puncta were observed, indicating that presynaptic TUJ1 +  and bassoon +  neurons are within 40nm of the postsynaptic PDGFRA + / homer +  PA cells, suggesting synaptic-width proximity between neuronal terminals and tumor cells. Using PANCOs containing DREADD-activatable neurons in close proximity to the tumor cells, we found that 24h (10μM) CNO treatment was sufficient to induce a 10-fold increase in neuronal activation (GFP/c-Fos + ) within the iNPC-GFP +  containing PANCOs ( Fig. 4D ). In contrast, CNO treatment of PANCOs devoid of hM3DqR-GFP +  iNPC-derived neurons had no effect on native neuron activation (c-Fos +  cells;  Supplemental Fig. S4C ), highlighting the specificity of the designer drug (CNO) for selective activation of integrated engineered neurons. Moreover, while 87% of activated c-Fos +  cells were NeuN +  neurons, PA cells were c-Fos neg  ( Supplemental Fig. S5A ), demonstrating that CNO-driven neuronal activation does not stimulate PA cells, similar to our prior findings using these PA cells in vitro ( Anastasaki et al. 2025 ). As iNPCs can spontaneously differentiate into either excitatory (glutamatergic) or inhibitory (GABAergic) neurons within the hCOs, we characterized the neuronal identity of hM3DqR-GFP +  iNPC-derived neurons in PANCOs. 80% of the iNPC-derived GFP +  neurons were glutamatergic (v-Glut 1 + ), while only 1% were GABAergic (GAD67 + ;  Fig. 5A – C ). Moreover, 58% of GFP +  neurons were activated by CNO and were c-Fos + , and among these neurons, only 1% were GABAergic ( Fig. 5C ). Together, these data support that CNO-activated iNPC-derived neurons are glutamatergic. Consistent with the presence of glutamatergic neurons, CNO induced a 6.9-fold increase in secreted glutamate after 24 hours ( Fig. 5D ), without affecting GABA levels ( Supplemental Fig S5B ). Next, we sought to determine whether glutamatergic neuron activation regulates PA cell proliferation within PANCOs. In these experiments, we evaluated PA cell proliferation (Ki67 +  cells) in PANCOs following vehicle or CNO treatment, with or without glutamate receptor (memantine) inhibition: CNO addition increased PA proliferation in both NF1- and Sp-PANCOs ( Fig 5E – F ,  Supplemental Fig. S5C – D ), whereas subsequent memantine administration restored PA cell proliferation to vehicle-treated control levels ( Fig. 5E – F ). Additionally, to ensure that the CNO-mediated activation was specific to the hM3DqR-GFP +  iNPC-derived neurons, we treated PANCOs without integrated hM3DqR-GFP +  iNPCs and found that CNO had no effect on PA cell proliferation ( Fig. 5E – F ). Since neuronal glutamate induces PA cell proliferation by ERK activation ( Anastasaki et al. 2025 ), we assessed pERK1/2 expression in PANCOs with and without integrated hM3DqR-GFP +  iNPCs following vehicle- or CNO treatment ( Fig. 5E ). We demonstrated that CNO-mediated glutamatergic neuron stimulation enhanced PA ERK activation (pERK1/2 expression) only in PANCOs containing hM3DqR-GFP +  iNPCs. Taken together, the close proximity of neuronal presynaptic proteins to tumoral postsynaptic proteins (revealed by confocal microscopy and puncta colocalization in PLA assays) supports the existence of structural synapses. Further ultrastructural and electrophysiological studies will be required to determine whether  bona fide  synapses or gap junctions are responsible for this unique neuron-tumor growth dependency. The finding that PA growth in PANCOs is regulated in a glutamate receptor (GluR)-dependent manner opens the door to the evaluation of more selective GluR antagonists currently in clinical use for children with epilepsy or mood disorders ( NCT03194906 ), as well as other neurotransmitter cell-extrinsic growth dependencies ( Venkataramani et al. 2019 ;  Venkatesh et al. 2019 ) ( Drexler et al. 2024 ;  Liu et al. 2024 ;  Barron et al. 2025 ). In addition, PANCOs could serve as in vitro platforms to dissect the mechanisms underlying the physical interaction between neurons and glioma cells involving tumor microtubes and gap junctions ( Venkataramani et al. 2022 ), enabling future investigations focused on the specificity of neuronal population-tumor interactions, the mechanisms mediating neuronal attraction to tumors, and the role of other brain stromal cell types in neuron regulation of PA growth (e.g., oligodendrocytes, astrocytes). In this regard, PANCOs can be engineered to incorporate additional cellular components, such as tumor-associated monocytes (TAMs), which are pivotal in regulating T cell infiltration ( Chatterjee et al. 2025 ), as well as regulating glioma cell growth ( Guo et al. 2020 ;  Chen et al. 2023 ). In this manner, PANCOs represent an accurate avatar amenable to the exploration of preclinical therapeutic agents, as well as basic science investigations, relevant to the management of this common pediatric brain tumor.
